cards.text: A {{c1::static member variable}} is shared by all objects of a class, with only {{c2::one copy}} existing regardless of how many objects are created.
Static member variables must be {{c1::defined}} outside the class definition (usually in a .cpp file), even if they are {{c2::declared}} inside the class.
To define a static member variable outside the class: {{c1::int ClassName::}}s_varName{{c2::{value}}};
Static member variables can be accessed using either {{c1::ClassName::varName}} or {{c2::object.varName}}, but the {{c1::class name}} syntax is preferred.
Since C++17, static constexpr member variables can be {{c1::initialized inside the class}} without requiring a separate definition.

cards.front: What is the difference between a static member variable and a regular member variable?
cards.back:
Static member variables:
  • Are shared by all objects of the class (one copy total)
  • Exist even if no objects are created
  • Regular member variables: Each object has its own copy

cards.front: Why must static member variables be defined outside the class?
cards.back: Because the class definition is just a blueprint and doesn't allocate memory. The external definition allocates actual storage for the static variable.

cards.question: Complete the static member variable definition.
cards.explanation: Define outside the class using ClassName::varName syntax. Do not use the static keyword in the definition.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Each time a Counter object is constructed, s_count is incremented. Since it's static (shared), all three constructors increment the same variable.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Static non-const member variables cannot be initialized inside the class (except for static constexpr).
cards.explanation: Need to declare in class and define outside: static int s_value; in class, then int Data::s_value{ 10 }; outside.

cards.concept: Common uses for static member variables
cards.explanation:
Static member variables are useful for:
  • Counting instances of a class
  • Storing configuration shared by all objects
  • Implementing singleton patterns
  • Sharing lookup tables or caches
  • Maintaining class-wide state

cards.back: A member variable shared by all instances of a class, with only one copy existing for the entire class
